title: Realtime Database
objectives: Choose RTDB vs Firestore for the task

sections.markdown: Realtime Database is Firebase's original database — a single, JSON-tree store synchronized in real time via WebSocket. Reads/writes stream to clients within milliseconds; presence and low-latency updates are its superpower.

rows: One shard until you shard manually

sections.markdown: Firestore is the default choice for new apps. Reach for RTDB only when you need ultra-low-latency presence/chat, or you're maintaining an existing RTDB codebase. RTDB's read charges by data volume, not doc count — very different cost model.
Nesting deeply and reading the whole tree — you pay for and download the entire subtree. **Flatten** data (denormalize) and use shallow references to keep reads small.
Turn on offline persistence for smoother UX. Security Rules use `.read/.write/.validate` expressions — different syntax from Firestore. Use `onDisconnect()` handlers to manage presence.

flashcards.back: One giant JSON tree; every path can be read or listened to.

flashcards.front: RTDB vs Firestore for new apps?
flashcards.back: Prefer Firestore unless you need ultra-low-latency chat/presence or have RTDB legacy.

flashcards.front: How do you avoid huge reads?
flashcards.back: Flatten (denormalize) data; read narrow subtrees; use shallow references.

quiz.explain: Single JSON tree, synchronized in real time.

quiz.q: The best fix for deep-tree reads is…
quiz.explain: Flat data lets you read only what you need.

challenges.text: Write and stream messages in a chat room path.
Flatten a nested tree; measure read savings.
Shard the DB across instances for large user bases.

interviewQuestions: RTDB vs Firestore — when use each?
